questions.text: My emotional responses tend to linger long after the triggering event has passed.
I like when the group collectively onboards new members.
I prefer relationships where I set the agenda.
I focus on getting the right answer rather than on how that answer will land socially.
I find that my physiological responses — heart rate, breathing, muscle tension — rarely fluctuate with my mood.
I tend not to notice when a person distances themselves from a previous statement in subtle ways.
I prefer that ground rules emerge from group discussion before activity begins.
I believe that taking someone's suffering seriously, even when it is complex or inconvenient, is a form of moral courage.
I find that my emotional reactions, even to genuinely significant events, stay within a range I can comfortably hold.
I find that my emotional steadiness during others' hardships allows me to remain present and constructive long after emotional engagement would have exhausted me.
My best thinking happens at unplanned moments, so I don't try to schedule it.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the reality that tests whether my ethical commitments are genuinely held or merely professed.
I detect when a person's social warmth has a ceiling that they never allow anyone past.
I find that the felt weight of another's suffering has a clarifying effect on my priorities that nothing else can replicate.
I routinely adapt my preferences to accommodate the needs of those around me.
I find that having a morning routine sets a productive tone for the entire day.
I tend to be unaware of when someone is matching my energy level to put me at ease.
I hold the view that personal ambition and drive are qualities to be proud of, not downplayed.
I pick up on when a person who normally speaks freely becomes uncharacteristically cautious.
I work until I'm done or until my energy shifts, without fixed start and end times.
I pick up on the difference between someone who is quiet by nature and someone who has been silenced by the group.
My emotional responses have a quality of proportion — they match the magnitude of the event.
I create redundancy in important systems so a single failure doesn't cause disaster.
I routinely stay late to help a colleague finish their work, even when mine is already done.
I tend to run on my own internal clock rather than watching the time closely.
I tend to save the best resources—time, energy, money—for my own priorities.
I review my goals regularly and adjust my plans to stay on track.
I enjoy knowing that a project can succeed regardless of whether I am personally driving it.
I experience the thrill of discovery — a new idea, a new place — as an almost dizzying rush.
I would give up a cherished personal goal if doing so served a greater collective need.
I find that I am someone who feels the full spectrum — there are no muted tones in my emotional palette.
I believe that personal achievement is the foundation upon which everything else in my life rests.
I believe that the separation of emotional content from decision-making content is the hallmark of clear thinking under pressure.
I think that analyzing emotionally difficult situations through a lens of detached reasoning is the method that produces the most impartial and well-balanced conclusions.
I tend to assert my position firmly in discussions.
I feel responsible for making sure the right things happen.
